title: Validity and Limits of Low Order Hybridization Expansion Approaches for Multi-Orbital Systems
authors: Dolev Goldberger, Ido Zemach, Lei Zhang, Yang Yu, Emanuel Gull, Guy Cohen, André Erpenbeck
abstract: Low-order hybridization expansion methods such as the non-crossing approximation (NCA) and the one-crossing approximation (OCA) are widely used impurity solvers in the study of strongly correlated systems, yet their accuracy in genuine multi-orbital settings remains poorly understood. Using the decoupled orbital limit as a controlled reference point, we derive analytic results connecting multi-orbital restricted propagators and Green's functions to their single-orbital counterparts, identify the diagrammatic mechanisms responsible for the breakdown of low-order methods in multi-orbital settings, and determine their regimes of applicability. Our central finding is that the accuracy of these methods is governed by the least correlated orbital: i.e., the orbital with the most rapidly decaying retarded Green's function. That orbital's properties are transferred to all other orbitals through a spurious coupling generated by the truncated expansion, thereby suppressing correlation-induced features such as the Kondo resonance. This occurs even in orbitals that are themselves strongly correlated within single-orbital calculations using the same approximation scheme. We confirm this numerically across representative two-orbital model systems in the steady-state, systematically identifying the parameter regimes in which low-order methods succeed or fail. Our results provide a practical guide for assessing when insights from single-orbital calculations carry over to multi-orbital settings, and serve as a benchmark for the development and validation of higher-order multi-orbital impurity solvers.
summary: This paper identifies why low-order hybridization expansion methods fail in multi-orbital settings by showing that the least correlated orbital unphysically suppresses correlations in all other orbitals. It provides a theoretical and numerical guide for determining when single-orbital intuition remains valid in multi-orbital systems.
main_problem: The paper investigates the accuracy and fundamental limitations of low-order hybridization expansion methods, such as NCA and OCA, when applied to multi-orbital quantum impurity systems.
main_result: The authors find that the accuracy of these methods is governed by the least correlated orbital; a spurious coupling is generated by the truncated expansion that transfers the properties of the rapidly decaying orbital to all others, suppressing features like the Kondo resonance.
method: The study uses an analytical approach based on a decoupled orbital limit as a reference point, combined with numerical verification using two-orbital model systems in the steady-state.
model_or_system: A general multi-orbital Anderson impurity model where an interacting impurity region is coupled to infinite noninteracting fermionic baths, allowing for arbitrary intra- and inter-orbital electron-electron interactions.
key_observables: Single-particle spectral functions, real-time retarded Green's functions, and the Kondo resonance peak height.
important_parameters: Hybridization strength (Gamma), inverse temperature (beta), Coulomb interaction (U), and the hybridization ratio between different orbitals.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the truncated hybridization expansion (NCA/OCA) and uses the decoupled orbital limit as a controlled benchmark.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows Feynman diagrams for NCA and OCA; Figure 2 compares diagrammatic structures of single-orbital vs multi-orbital NCA; Figures 6-9 show spectral functions, Kondo peak suppression, and the decay of retarded Green's functions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the multi-orbital framework, establishes the analytical connection between multi-orbital and single-orbital propagators in the decoupled limit, identifies the mechanism of spurious coupling, and provides numerical validation through two-orbital steady-state calculations.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for researchers in many-body dynamics and open quantum systems as it identifies a fundamental failure mode in common impurity solvers used to model the dynamics of interacting subsystems coupled to environments.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. Feynman diagram representation of contributions retained and omitted at the NCA level. Left: factorized product of single-orbital descriptions, which applies in the decoupled-orbital limit considered in this work. Right: corresponding multi- orbital formulation for the same system. Solid lines denote restricted impurity propagators, φβ α(t) or Φβ′β α (t, t′), depending on whether one or two branches of the Keldysh contour are involved. Each vertex at time τi corresponds to an insertion of the system-bath coupling HSB in the hybridization expansion, while wiggly lines represent hybridization functions encoding the coupling to the bath.

FIG. 3. Schematic illustration of the model systems used in our numerical calculations, consisting of two spinful orbitals, each coupled to its own bath. The orbital with interaction strength U1 is the primary orbital of interest, while the sec- ond orbital with interaction strength U2 serves as an addi- tional spectator orbital, rendering the setup a genuine multi- orbital system. In Sec. IV B 2, the second orbital is taken to be noninteracting (U2 = 0), representing a correlated orbital embedded in a weakly correlated or effectively noninteracting environment. In Sec. IV B 3 and IV B 4, we instead consider U2 = U1, corresponding to a system in which multiple inter- acting orbitals jointly...

FIG. 4. Spectral function of a two-orbital system consist- ing of one interacting orbital U1 = 8Γ and one noninteracting orbital U2 = 0, as illustrated in Fig. 3. The hybridization of the noninteracting orbital is varied over Γ0 = 10−4Γ . . . 1.0Γ, while the coupling of the interacting orbital is kept fixed at ΓU = Γ. The inverse temperature of the system is β = 100/Γ. (a) Spectral function of the interacting orbital from a multi- orbital calculation. Top: NCA results. Bottom: OCA results. The dashed black line denotes the corresponding single-orbital calculation. Insets provide a close up on the Kondo peak. (b) Kondo peak height as a function of Γ0. Orange and blue curves show NCA and OCA...

FIG. 5. Retarded Green’s functions at the NCA level for the same system and parameters as shown in Fig. 4. Top: nonin- teracting orbital for varying Γ0 from the single-orbital calcu- lation. Bottom: interacting orbital from the multi-orbital cal- culation for the same parameters. In both panels, the dashed black line corresponds to the single-orbital result for the in- teracting orbital which serves as a reference point.

FIG. 6. Spectral function of a two-orbital system consisting of two interacting orbitals with U1 = U2 = 8Γ, as illustrated in Fig. 3, calculated at the NCA level. The hybridization of the the second orbital is varied over ΓU2 = 10−4Γ . . . 1.0Γ, while the coupling of the interacting orbital is kept fixed at ΓU1 = Γ. The inverse temperature of the system is β = 100/Γ. (a) Spectral function for both orbitals from a multi-orbital calculation. Top: Results for the first orbital U1 with constant coupling to the baths. Bottom: Results for the second orbital U2 with variable coupling to the baths ΓU2.
